claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 9 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 9 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 9 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start by setting up your assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let me help you check your eligibility for Child Benefit. To start, I need to know about the child (or children) you're asking about.

**First question: How old is your child?**

This is important because:
- Children **under 16** may automatically qualify
- Children **aged 16-19** may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children **aged 20 or over** generally don't qualify

Please tell me your child's age (and if you have multiple children, please tell me about each of them).

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi! Thanks for helping me with this. I have two children - Alex is 8 years old and Blake is 9 years old. They both live at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! That's very helpful. You have:
- **Alex** - 8 years old
- **Blake** - 9 years old

Both children are under 16, which is good news as they fall into the automatic qualification age category. Let me continue with the assessment for children under 16.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now, the next check:

**Are Alex and Blake working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?**

Since they're only 8 and 9 years old, I expect they're not in paid employment, but I need to confirm this for the assessment. Please confirm that neither Alex nor Blake is working 24 or more hours per week in paid work.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right - neither of them work at all. They're just kids, so no paid work for either Alex or Blake.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, thank you for confirming that! Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Next question:

**Are Alex and Blake NOT receiving any benefits in their own right?**

This means benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit that would be claimed in the child's own name (not yours as the parent). 

Given their ages (8 and 9), this is typically not the case, but I need to confirm that neither Alex nor Blake is receiving benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them receives any benefits in their own name. They're just regular kids - no ESA or Universal Credit or anything like that for them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent, thank you! Let me move to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Next question:

**Are either Alex or Blake in an apprenticeship?**

At ages 8 and 9, this is highly unlikely, but I need to confirm they are NOT in any type of apprenticeship program (as certain apprenticeships can disqualify children from Child Benefit eligibility).

Can you confirm that neither child is in an apprenticeship?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, definitely not - they're way too young for that! Neither Alex nor Blake is in any apprenticeship. They're still in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That makes sense. Let me continue to the next stage of the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now we need to check your residency status. 

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements for Child Benefit?**

You can qualify if ANY of the following applies to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK** (this is the most common path)
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold)
4. You have pre-settled status and are actively seeking work
5. You have pre-settled status with sufficient resources to support your family
6. You have pre-settled status and are studying
7. You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national
8. You're a Crown servant living abroad

**Do any of these apply to you?** The simplest path is if you normally live in the UK - is that the case?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK. I'm in Manchester with the kids.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That satisfies the residency requirement. Let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now I need to confirm your responsibility for Alex and Blake.

**Are you responsible for both Alex and Blake?**

You mentioned they live at home with you in Manchester, which means you meet the responsibility requirement. Just to confirm:

- **Do Alex and Blake live with you?** (which you've already indicated they do)

Or alternatively, if they didn't live with you, you could qualify by contributing to their upkeep, but since they live with you, that clearly establishes responsibility.

Can you confirm that both children live with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - both Alex and Blake live with me at home.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to check about other potential claimants.

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex or Blake?**

For example:
- Another parent or guardian
- An ex-partner
- A grandparent or other relative

Only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child. If someone else is claiming, you would need to arrange for them to stop their claim before you can claim.

Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for either of them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me move to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check the children's living situation.

**Are Alex and Blake:**
- In hospital (for an extended stay)?
- In care (local authority care)?
- Being fostered?
- Or living normally at home with you?

You mentioned they live at home with you, so I believe the answer is that they're living normally at home, but I need to confirm that neither child is currently in hospital for a long stay, in local authority care, or in a fostering arrangement.

Can you confirm they're just living normally at home with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's correct - they're both just living at home with me. No hospital stays, not in care, and not fostered. Just a normal home situation.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Just one final check:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex and Blake's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, the council is not contributing financially to their housing or upkeep. Since they live with you in a normal home situation, I expect this is the case, but I need to confirm that the local council is not providing any financial support for their accommodation or maintenance.

Can you confirm that the council is NOT paying for their accommodation or maintenance?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right - the council isn't paying anything towards their accommodation or maintenance. I'm supporting them myself.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Wonderful! Let me get the final outcome for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## 🎉 Great News - You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit!

**Result: ELIGIBLE** ✓

You meet all the Child Benefit eligibility requirements for both **Alex** and **Blake**.

### Summary:
- ✓ Both children are under 16 years old
- ✓ Neither child is in paid work (24+ hours/week)
- ✓ Neither child receives benefits in their own right
- ✓ Neither child is in a disqualifying apprenticeship
- ✓ You meet UK residency requirements (living in Manchester)
- ✓ You have responsibility for both children (they live with you)
- ✓ No one else is claiming Child Benefit for them
- ✓ They live in a normal home situation
- ✓ The council is not paying for their accommodation or maintenance

### Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and both children
3. **Provide birth certificates** (or adoption certificates) for Alex and Blake

### Important Information:

⚠️ **High Income Child Benefit Charge**: If you or your partner earns over £60,000 per year, you may need to pay a tax charge. However, you can still claim - some people claim anyway to protect their National Insurance credits for State Pension.

💷 **Payment**: Child Benefit is usually paid every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits).

📋 **How to apply**: Visit https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

Do you have any questions about the application process or anything else?

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old (under 16), not in paid work, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship, lives with the claimant in the UK, no other person is claiming for them, living normally at home, and the council is not paying towards their accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.

Blake is 9 years old (under 16), not in paid work, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship, lives with the claimant in the UK, no other person is claiming for them, living normally at home, and the council is not paying towards their accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for both children (Alex, age 8, and Blake, age 9). Both children meet all qualifying criteria: they are under 16, not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in apprenticeships, live with the claimant in Manchester, no one else is claiming for them, they live in a normal home situation, and the council does not pay for their accommodation or maintenance. The claimant meets residency requirements by normally living in the UK and has responsibility for both children as they live together.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements

All eligibility criteria are met.

Blake is 9 years old (under 16), not in paid work, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship, lives with the claimant in the UK, no other person is claiming for them, living normally at home, and the council is not paying towards their accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for both children (Alex, age 8, and Blake, age 9). Both children meet all qualifying criteria: they are under 16, not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in apprenticeships, live with the claimant in Manchester, no one else is claiming for them, they live in a normal home situation, and the council does not pay for their accommodation or maintenance. The claimant meets residency requirements by normally living in the UK and has responsibility for both children as they live together.
